facts.text: Cortland West sits in the glaciated Appalachian Plateau, midway between Syracuse and Binghamton, marking the southeastern gateway to the Finger Lakes.
The area that is now Cortland West was first settled by white pioneers in 1791, when Amos Todd and others paddled up the Tioughnioga River from Connecticut.
Cortland County, which contains Cortland West, was formed in 1808 from the southern half of Onondaga County and named after Pierre Van Cortlandt, a key figure in New York’s first constitution.
During the American Revolution, the land that now makes up Cortland West was part of the Military Tract, a 1¼‑million‑acre reward for soldiers who defended the state’s western frontier.
In the early 1790s, the state built a road from Oxford through what is now Cortland West to Cayuga Lake, a major driver of settlement in the area.
